planting.note: Root cuttings or plant container-grown vines after all frost danger has passed; in zones 9–11 plant year-round in frost-free periods. Provide a sturdy trellis or fence to support the vigorous climbing vine.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun and provide a strong trellis or chain-link fence; the purple passion fruit vine grows rapidly and requires physical support for the tendrils. Unlike the cold-hardy native maypop (P. incarnata), this is a true tropical vine that produces the familiar large, purple, wrinkled fruits of commerce, intensely aromatic with sweet-tart yellow pulp. Fruits ripen and fall to the ground when ready — simply collect them from the ground. In zones 9b–11 plants produce heavily twice yearly, in spring and fall. The spectacular 3-inch flowers with purple, white, and yellow corona filaments are among the most intricate in the plant world and are visited by bumblebees, which are the most effective pollinators.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in full sun after soil has warmed; custard apple prefers well-drained soil and does not tolerate waterlogging. Grafted trees begin fruiting in 3–5 years, while seed-grown trees take 5–7 years.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained soil in full sun; custard apple is a deciduous to semi-evergreen Annona tree producing round to heart-shaped yellow-green to brownish fruits 3–4 inches in diameter with a reticulated (net-patterned) skin. The creamy white flesh inside is sweeter and softer than sugar apple, with a custard-like texture and a mild, slightly musky flavor. It is the Annona most widely grown across tropical Latin America, the Caribbean, and South Asia, where it is known variously as bull's heart, corazon, nona, and anon. Hand-pollinate flowers using a small brush in the morning to transfer pollen between flowers on the same tree; like other Annona species, the stigma and anther are not receptive at the same time.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit
Morton, J. Fruits of Warm Climates, 1987

planting.note: Plant small tubers 2–3 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil in spring; groundnut tubers grow on stringed rhizomes and the connected tubers expand over multiple years. Provide a trellis or shrubs for the twining vine to climb.

directions: Plant tubers in moist, rich soil in partial shade to full sun, near a trellis or fence the vine can climb; American groundnut is a nitrogen-fixing native vine whose starchy, walnut-sized tubers strung on underground rhizomes like beads were a critically important food source for Eastern Woodland indigenous peoples and the early European settlers who learned to use them. The tubers are boiled, baked, or fried and have a mild, chestnut-like flavor. Unlike many novel perennial vegetables, groundnut produces edible tubers reliably in its first year. The fragrant, rose-purple flowers in late summer attract native bees. It thrives in moist sites near streams and pond margins, and in zone 9 it may go dormant in summer heat.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in moist, humus-rich soil in partial shade after last frost; in zones 9–11 plants can be grown outdoors as perennials. In colder climates grow as a container plant and bring indoors for winter.

directions: Plant rhizomes in rich, moist, well-drained soil in partial shade; grains of paradise is a West African ginger relative producing striking orchid-like pink to purple flowers at the base of the plant, followed by red oval fruits each containing 60–100 small brown seeds. The seeds have a complex, layered flavor combining warm pepper heat, floral cardamom, and citrus — used historically in European medieval cooking as a pepper substitute and today experiencing a culinary revival in craft brewing, gin production, and spice-forward cooking. In Ghana it is known as efom wisa and is used in traditional stews and spice blends. The ornamental foliage and unusual basal flowers make this a beautiful specimen plant for warm-climate shade gardens.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in fall in partial to full shade; three-cornered garlic is one of the few alliums that thrives in shade and tolerates competition from tree roots. NOTE: this species has naturalized invasively in parts of California and Pacific coastal regions; check local invasive plant lists before planting.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in moist, well-drained soil in partial shade to full shade; three-cornered garlic is named for its distinctively triangular stem cross-section, visible when the stem is cut. This Mediterranean allium is one of the most useful for shade gardens because it genuinely thrives under trees where garlic and chives fail. The small white bell flowers in spring are pendulous and nodding, borne in a one-sided umbel, and all parts — leaves, stems, flowers, and bulbs — have a mild garlic flavor and are fully edible. Leaves can be harvested like chives throughout fall and winter in mild climates; use them raw in salads, cooked in omelets, or as a mild garlic substitute wherever a delicate garlic presence is wanted.
